Marketing Analytics Dashboard

This dashboard is created using the weekly sales of 44 stock-keeping units (SKUs) from a tech-gadget e-commerce retailer over a period of 100 weeks, from October 2016 to September 2018.
Tips: Click and drag the portion of some graphs of your choice to zoom in and out. Double-click to reset. Select different SKUs from the dropdown to show different forecast.
Edward Lim, Fabricio Rodriguez Peña, Rafia Al-Jassim, Rathes Waran, Wenyuan Yun

Welcome to the Marketing Analytics Dashboard

This dashboard analyses 44 SKUs from a tech-gadget e-commerce retailer across 100 weeks of sales data. It is organised into three analytical tabs. Here is how to navigate and interpret each one:

1. Demand Forecasting

Select any SKU from the dropdown to see its historical sales and predicted future demand. Three machine learning models (Linear Regression, Random Forest, and Neural Network) compete to forecast sales — the best-performing model is automatically highlighted.

  • Interpretation box — A plain-English summary tells you the expected demand, the trend direction, and the confidence range.
  • Forecast chart — The shaded band around the best model’s forecast is the 95% confidence interval: the wider it gets, the more uncertain the prediction.
  • Forecast weeks slider — Adjust to look further ahead (2–12 weeks).
  • Test size slider — Controls how many of the most recent weeks are held out from training and used as evaluation data. A smaller test size (4–6) gives the models more training data but fewer weeks to visually compare predictions against reality. A larger test size (12–16) provides a longer comparison window but less training data. Use this to stress-test the models — if a model scores well at test size 8 but poorly at 14, it may be overfitting. Consistent results across different test sizes build confidence in the forecast.
  • Heatmap — Gives a quick portfolio-level view of predicted demand across all SKUs.

2. Promotion Effectiveness

This tab answers: “Do feature promotions actually boost sales, and by how much?”

  • Portfolio KPIs — Show how many SKUs have a statistically significant promotion effect.
  • Per-SKU detail — Select a SKU to see its promotion coefficient (β₂), the estimated sales lift, and incremental units generated per promo week.
  • Lift chart — Ranks all SKUs by incremental sales. Blue bars = significant positive lift; red = significant negative; grey = not significant.
  • ★ symbol — Indicates statistical significance (p < 0.05), meaning we can be confident the effect is real.

3. Price Elasticity

This tab answers: “How sensitive is each SKU’s demand to price changes?”

  • Elasticity (ε) — A value of −2 means a 10% price increase causes a ~20% drop in demand. Values below −1 are “elastic” (price-sensitive); above −1 are “inelastic.”
  • Demand & Revenue curves — Show the trade-off between price and volume. The ★ on the revenue curve marks the revenue-maximising price.
  • Scenario table — Shows the exact impact of raising or lowering price by 5–30%.
  • Strategy column — Recommends whether to consider raising or lowering price based on elasticity.

General Tips

  • Click and drag on any Plotly chart to zoom in. Double-click to reset.
  • Hover over data points for detailed tooltips.
  • Use the SKU dropdowns on each tab to switch between products.
  • Green numbers indicate positive outcomes; red indicates negative.
4
8

Forecast Detail

All-SKU Forecast Heatmap (Random Forest)

Model Comparison

Diagnostics

Our Methodology — Demand Forecasting

Data: 44 SKUs × 98 weeks from the processed dataset with lagged prices, trend, month dummies, and one-hot categoricals.

Train/test split: Last N weeks held out (temporal split — no data leakage).

Models:

  • Linear Regression — OLS baseline on raw features
  • Random Forest — 200 trees, max_depth=8, min_samples_leaf=4
  • Neural Network (MLP) — 2 hidden layers (64→32), scaled inputs, early stopping

Confidence intervals: Training residual σ × 1.96 × √(step) — widens with forecast horizon. 95% level.

Best model selection: Lowest MAE on the held-out test set.

Reference: Cohen, M.C., Gras, P.E., Pentecoste, A., & Zhang, R. (2022). Demand Prediction in Retail. Springer SSCM 14.

Portfolio Promotion Overview

Incremental Sales Lift — All SKUs

Promotion Effectiveness Summary

Our Methodology — Feature Promotion Effectiveness

Model: The Scan*Pro log-log OLS model (fitted per SKU) includes a binary feat_main_page indicator. The estimated coefficient β₂ captures the multiplicative lift in demand when a feature promotion is active.

Incremental sales: Since the model is semi-log in the promotion variable, the lift factor is eβ₂, meaning weekly sales increase by a factor of eβ₂ during promotion weeks. Incremental units per week = Q₀ × (eβ₂ − 1).

Significance: A p-value < 0.05 on β₂ indicates the promotion effect is statistically distinguishable from zero at the 95% confidence level.

Reference: Van Heerde, H.J., Leeflang, P.S.H., & Wittink, D.R. (2004). Decomposing the Sales Promotion Bump. Marketing Science, 23(3).

Revenue Impact Heatmap — All SKUs × All Scenarios

All-SKU Price Strategy Summary

Our Methodology — Price Elasticity

Data: 44 SKUs × 98 weeks from data_processed.csv (lagged prices, trend, month dummies, one-hot categoricals). SKU names from data_raw.csv.

Model: Scan*Pro log-log OLS fitted per SKU:

log(sales) = β₀ + β₁·log(price) + β₂·feat_main_page + β₃·trend + Σγₘ·monthₘ + ε

The price coefficient β₁ is the price elasticity directly.

Scenario simulation: Q_new = Q₀ × (P_new/P₀)^ε  ·  R_new = P_new × Q_new

  • Elastic (|ε| > 1): Lowering price grows revenue
  • Inelastic (|ε| < 1): Raising price grows revenue

Reference: Cohen, M.C., Gras, P.E., Pentecoste, A., & Zhang, R. (2022). Demand Prediction in Retail. Springer SSCM 14.

Marketing Analytics Dashboard · Demand Forecasting (scikit-learn) + Price Elasticity (Scan*Pro OLS)
